letters.label: Letter One
variants:
My dear.

I found the train.
It's real.

Yet still I hold doubts.


They say the train carries what you need.

No one says what exactly.
I only hope our need isn't too great.

A short while aboard, and it has no end.

— Tomas

I told my young one, I'd be back by harvest.

I shouldn't have promised a season. I don't know if this place has them.

But this train holds our only hope.

— Tomas

letters.label: Letter Two
variants:
My young one.

There are dead men on this train. They walk.

I know how that sounds.

Da had to fight one.


My hands know seed and soil.
Today they learned a hoe swings two ways.

I was afraid. I did it anyway. That is what brave is.

It went down. I am not hurt.

Da is still coming home.

— Da

My young one.

I have done a thing I can't undo. A man came at me, only he wasn't a man anymore.

I used what was in my hands.


You'd have closed your eyes. I kept mine open. One of us had to.

After, my hands shook like yours do in the cold.

I held them till they stopped. Then I kept walking.

— Da

letters.label: Letter Three
variants:
My dear.

A rat spoke to me today. In a little vest.

I'm not losing my head. Others see him too.

He gave me paper, and a riddle.


"A seed doesn't chase the spring," he said. "It waits. The young learn it first."

Then he wished me well, and looked sad. I thanked him and kept walking.

— Tomas

My dear.

I met the rat again. He talks like a gentleman, which is strange on a rat.

I asked him which way to the front.


"There's no front for the walking," he said. "Only the ones who stop get found. The little ones know."

I don't have time to stop. He wished me well anyway.

— Tomas

letters.label: Letter Four
variants:
My dear.

The train is full of locked boxes. Every car has them.

Any one could hold what we need. So I open them all.


Gold. Iron. Enough to buy the village back twice.

I take it all. It's for you, I say.

I'll stop at the right box. I keep saying that too.

— Tomas

My dear.

I keep meaning to walk on. Then there's another box, and another.

A man could fill a barn with what I've left behind.


I take only what fits the pack now. The pack is always full.

Yesterday I couldn't recall what I came aboard to find.

Only that I'd know it. So I keep opening boxes.

— Tomas

letters.label: Letter Five
variants:
i don't know how long i've been here.

the wheels make three notes. i count those instead of days.

i tried to say the village name aloud. it wouldn't come.


i still know the orange book. small. orange. she never put it down.

if i can hold onto that, i can hold the rest.

can't i.

— tomas

i catch myself talking. out loud. no one there.

i look at my hands and they aren't a farmer's. i don't know whose they are.


i keep walking because stopping feels like dying.

the rat said the opposite. the rat. did i dream the rat.

i forget what i'm walking toward. i walk toward it anyway.

— tomas

letters.label: Letter Six
variants:
My dear.

There's a child ahead. Far up the car.

She holds something orange. She won't put it down.

My chest. My chest.


Is it her? Could it be, after all this?

Who is the woman beside her? Where is her mother? Why would a child be here at all?

Or have I lost my mind, and is the train just being kind?

— Da

My dear.

A child, ahead in the car. A woman I don't know sits with her.

The child has something orange in her lap.

I know that orange.


What if I walk up and it isn't her? What is left of me then?

And what if it is? Why is she here? Where are you? And who is that woman?

I've stopped walking. After all this time, I've stopped.

— Tomas
